mnemonics.title: E-ncoder D-ecoder R-econstruction (EDR)
mnemonics.content: EDR reminds us: Encode compresses data, Decode reconstructs it, and Reconstruction loss measures how well we recovered the original input.

mnemonics.title: U-NDER - Undercomplete Does Encoding Reducing
mnemonics.content: U-NDER: Undercomplete autoencoders force dimensionality reduction by learning compressed representations in the bottleneck (latent space).

mnemonics.title: V-A-E: Very Artistic Encoder
mnemonics.content: VAE (Variational Autoencoder) creates artistic, smooth interpolations by learning probability distributions rather than fixed points in latent space.

mnemonics.title: DAE: Destroy And Encode
mnemonics.content: Denoising Autoencoders (DAE) deliberately destroy/add noise to input, then learn to encode and reconstruct the clean original - teaching robustness.

mnemonics.title: L-B-L: Latent Bottle Limits Information
mnemonics.content: The Latent bottleneck limits information flow, forcing the network to learn the most essential features (principal components) of the data.

acronyms.usage: The base model consisting of encoder and decoder that learns to reconstruct its input
When you need to generate new data samples; learns probability distribution for creative generation
When learning robust features from corrupted data or for image denoising tasks
When you want to learn interpretable features with sparsity constraints
When learning robust features that are less sensitive to small changes in input
Technique for sampling from the learned latent representation
Measures difference between input and reconstructed output; typically MSE or cross-entropy

visualTips.visualization: Picture a funnel: wide at the top (input), narrowing down to a narrow neck (latent/bottleneck), then widening again to the output. Data gets squeezed through the narrow part.
Imagine a compressed 'essence' cube - all the important features of the input squeezed into fewer dimensions, like extracting the DNA of the data.
Think of photocopying: input is the original document, encoder makes a summary, decoder recreates a copy from that summary.
Undercomplete = narrow hallway forcing you to squeeze through (compression). Overcomplete = wide hallway with many paths, can memorize instead of compress.
Think of a cloudy sphere in latent space: instead of one point, you have a distribution of possible points, allowing smooth interpolation when generating new samples.

analogies.analogy: Like a JPEG image compressor: it removes redundant information, keeps essential features (edges, shapes), and reconstructs a close approximation of the original image with fewer bits.
Like a skilled editor for a movie: watches the full film, creates a detailed but condensed script (bottleneck), from which another team reconstructs the movie.
Like a translator who intentionally listens to a noisy, staticky radio transmission and learns to fill in the missing words to reconstruct the original message accurately.

Like learning the 'essence' of a face: instead of memorizing specific faces, you learn what makes a face - eye spacing, nose shape - allowing you to generate NEW faces by combining these learned features.
Like marking an exam: the input is the answer key, the reconstruction is the student's answer, and reconstruction loss is the difference/penalty score showing how much they got wrong.
